title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
While updating my website landing page, I started thinking about a simple question: could a small change in wording lead to more newsletter sign-ups? The page includes a call to action (CTA), so I decided to compare two versions. CTA A says ŌĆ£Sign up for our newsletter here!ŌĆØ while CTA B says ŌĆ£Stay up to date by signing up!ŌĆØ
To figure out which one performs better, I use an A/B test. Visitors are randomly shown either CTA A or CTA B, and I compare the sign-up rates between the two groups. Random assignment matters because it helps keep the groups comparable, so any difference in conversion is more likely to come from the CTA wording rather than from differences in the visitors themselves.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
While updating my website landing page, I started thinking about a simple question: could a small change in wording lead to more newsletter sign-ups? The page includes a call to action (CTA), so I decided to compare two versions. CTA A says ŌĆ£Sign up for our newsletter here!ŌĆØ while CTA B says ŌĆ£Stay up to date by signing up!ŌĆØ
To figure out which one performs better, I use an A/B test. Visitors are randomly shown either CTA A or CTA B, and I compare the sign-up rates between the two groups. Random assignment matters because it helps keep the groups comparable, so any difference in conversion is more likely to come from the CTA wording rather than from differences in the visitors themselves.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
Statistically, each visitor has a binary outcome: signup (1) or no signup (0). So each observation can be modeled as a Bernoulli random variable.

Let (_A) be the signup probability for visitors who see CTA A.
Let (_B) be the signup probability for visitors who see CTA B.

The parameter I care about is the difference in signup probabilities:
[ = _A - _B ]
Its sample estimator is the difference in sample means:
[ = X_A - X_B ]
Since (X{0,1}), each sample mean is just a sample proportion, so this is also (= _A - _B).

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In real A/B tests, we do not know the true values of (_A) and (_B). For this exercise, I set them myself so I can study how the estimator behaves when the truth is known.
I use:
[ _A = 0.22,_B = 0.18,= 0.04 ]
Then I draw 1,000 observations from each Bernoulli distribution.

n <- 1000
pi_A <- 0.22
pi_B <- 0.18
true_theta <- pi_A - pi_B

x_A <- rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = pi_A)
x_B <- rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = pi_B)

sim_df <- tibble(
  group = rep(c("A", "B"), each = n),
  signup = c(x_A, x_B)
)

summary_tbl <- sim_df %>%
  group_by(group) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    signup_rate = mean(signup),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(signup_rate = round(signup_rate, 3))

summary_tbl %>%
  render_table(title = "Simulated A/B Data")







Simulated A/B Data


group
n
signup_rate




A
1000
0.223


B
1000
0.179

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers (LLN) says that as sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. In this setting, LLN is why we expect () to get closer to the true () with enough data.
Following the assignment instructions, I compute element-wise differences (d_i = X_{A,i} - X_{B,i}), then plot the cumulative average (running mean) over the first 1, 2, ŌĆ”, 1,000 observations.









The curve is noisy early on, but it stabilizes as more observations are included and moves toward 0.04. That is exactly what LLN predicts.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
Once I have a point estimate, the next question is precision. Bootstrap provides a practical way to estimate standard errors by resampling from observed data.
I repeatedly sample with replacement from each group, recompute (), and then use the standard deviation of those bootstrap estimates as the bootstrap SE.

B <- 1000

boot_theta <- replicate(B, {
  xA_star <- sample(x_A, size = n, replace = TRUE)
  xB_star <- sample(x_B, size = n, replace = TRUE)
  mean(xA_star) - mean(xB_star)
})

boot_se <- sd(boot_theta)

pA_hat <- mean(x_A)
pB_hat <- mean(x_B)

analytic_se <- sqrt(pA_hat * (1 - pA_hat) / n + pB_hat * (1 - pB_hat) / n)

theta_hat <- pA_hat - pB_hat

ci_low <- theta_hat - 1.96 * boot_se
ci_high <- theta_hat + 1.96 * boot_se

se_tbl <- tibble(
  metric = c("Estimate (theta_hat)", "Bootstrap SE", "Analytical SE", "95% CI (lower)", "95% CI (upper)"),
  value = c(theta_hat, boot_se, analytic_se, ci_low, ci_high)
) %>%
  mutate(value = round(value, 4))

se_tbl %>%
  render_table(title = "Precision of the A/B Estimate")







Precision of the A/B Estimate


metric
value




Estimate (theta_hat)
0.0440


Bootstrap SE
0.0171


Analytical SE
0.0179


95% CI (lower)
0.0106


95% CI (upper)
0.0774
















The bootstrap SE and analytical SE are usually very close, and they are close here as well. Using the bootstrap SE, I construct a 95% confidence interval for ().

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) says that even when raw data are not Normal (here they are Bernoulli), sampling distributions of sample means become approximately Normal as sample size increases.
I simulate sampling distributions of () for (n {25, 50, 100, 500}), using 1,000 repetitions per sample size.









At (n=25), the histogram is rougher and less smooth. As (n) grows, the distribution becomes more bell-shaped, which is the CLT pattern we expect.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
Given the CLT, I can run a formal test of:
[ H_0: = 0, H_1: ]
with test statistic:
[ z = ]
Under (H_0), () is centered at 0 and approximately Normal in large samples. Dividing by its SE standardizes it, giving a statistic that is approximately standard Normal, which allows direct p-value calculation.
People often call this a t-test in practice, but with Bernoulli outcomes and CLT-based approximation, this setup is closer to a z-test. In large samples, the practical difference is minimal.

z_stat <- theta_hat / analytic_se
p_val <- 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(z_stat)))

ht_tbl <- tibble(
  quantity = c("theta_hat", "SE(theta_hat)", "z statistic", "two-sided p-value"),
  value = c(theta_hat, analytic_se, z_stat, p_val)
) %>%
  mutate(value = round(value, 5))

ht_tbl %>%
  render_table(title = "Two-Sided Hypothesis Test (Normal Reference)")







Two-Sided Hypothesis Test (Normal Reference)


quantity
value




theta_hat
0.04400


SE(theta_hat)
0.01789


z statistic
2.45879


two-sided p-value
0.01394







If the p-value is below 0.05, I reject (H_0) and conclude the signup rates differ statistically. Otherwise, I do not have enough evidence to rule out no true difference.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
The two-sample test is mathematically equivalent to a simple regression:
[ Y_i = _0 + _1 D_i + _i ]
where (Y_i) is signup (0/1), and (D_i=1) for CTA A, (D_i=0) for CTA B.

(_0): mean signup rate in group B ((_B))
(_1): mean difference between A and B ((_A - _B = ))


reg_df <- tibble(
  Y = c(x_A, x_B),
  D = c(rep(1, n), rep(0, n))
)

fit <- lm(Y ~ D, data = reg_df)
fit_tidy <- tidy(fit)

reg_main <- fit_tidy %>%
  filter(term == "D") %>%
  transmute(
    estimate = estimate,
    std_error = std.error,
    t_stat = statistic,
    p_value = p.value
  )

compare_tbl <- tibble(
  method = c("Difference in means", "Regression coefficient on D"),
  estimate = c(theta_hat, reg_main$estimate),
  se = c(analytic_se, reg_main$std_error),
  statistic = c(z_stat, reg_main$t_stat),
  p_value = c(p_val, reg_main$p_value)
) %>%
  mutate(across(c(estimate, se, statistic, p_value), ~ round(.x, 5)))

compare_tbl %>%
  render_table(title = "Two-Sample Test vs. Regression Formulation")







Two-Sample Test vs. Regression Formulation


method
estimate
se
statistic
p_value




Difference in means
0.044
0.01789
2.45879
0.01394


Regression coefficient on D
0.044
0.01790
2.45756
0.01407







The two approaches should be very close. Small differences can come from standard error conventions (separate-variance formula vs default OLS assumptions). The practical value of this equivalence is that regression frameworks scale naturally to controls, interactions, and more complex experiments.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
Now for a common real-world issue: peeking at results repeatedly before data collection is complete.
If each interim check uses (), every peek is another chance for a false positive. So the overall false positive probability across many peeks is higher than 5%.
I simulate the null case ((_A = _B = 0.20)): in each experiment, I generate 1,000 observations per group, test after every 100 observations per group (10 peeks total), and record whether any p-value is below 0.05. I repeat this for 10,000 experiments.

set.seed(20260414)

R_peek <- 10000
peek_ns <- seq(100, 1000, by = 100)
pi_null <- 0.20

one_experiment_any_sig <- function() {
  a <- rbinom(1000, 1, pi_null)
  b <- rbinom(1000, 1, pi_null)

  for (nn in peek_ns) {
    a_n <- a[1:nn]
    b_n <- b[1:nn]
    th <- mean(a_n) - mean(b_n)
    se <- sqrt(mean(a_n) * (1 - mean(a_n)) / nn + mean(b_n) * (1 - mean(b_n)) / nn)

    if (se == 0) next

    z <- th / se
    p <- 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(z)))

    if (p < 0.05) return(TRUE)
  }
  FALSE
}

any_sig <- replicate(R_peek, one_experiment_any_sig())
fp_rate_peek <- mean(any_sig)

single_test_fp <- 0.05

peek_tbl <- tibble(
  scenario = c("Single test at alpha = 0.05", "Peeking 10 times (every +100 users/group)"),
  false_positive_rate = c(single_test_fp, fp_rate_peek)
) %>%
  mutate(false_positive_rate = scales::percent(false_positive_rate, accuracy = 0.1))

peek_tbl %>%
  render_table(title = "False Positive Rate Inflation from Peeking")







False Positive Rate Inflation from Peeking


scenario
false_positive_rate




Single test at alpha = 0.05
5.0%


Peeking 10 times (every +100 users/group)
19.8%







The peeking false positive rate is typically much higher than 5%. In practical terms, if stopping rules are not pre-registered (or corrected for repeated looks), A/B test decisions become too optimistic.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Closing Notes
My three main takeaways from this exercise are:

Point estimates are only the beginning; uncertainty (SEs and confidence intervals) is essential.
CLT makes many large-sample tools workable, but assumptions and approximations still matter.
Experimental design choices (like peeking) directly affect error rates, not just final p-values.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Card and Krueger (1994) study whether the April 1, 1992 minimum wage increase in New Jersey (from $4.25 to $5.05) reduced fast-food employment. They compare fast-food stores in New Jersey to stores in eastern Pennsylvania, where the minimum wage did not change.
The core empirical design is a difference-in-differences (DID): compare the change in employment in New Jersey before vs.┬Āafter the policy to the same before-after change in Pennsylvania.
Note on replication targets: the assignment emphasizes matching point estimates closely. Standard errors can differ across replications because of choices about sample restrictions and handling of temporarily closed stores.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Card and Krueger (1994) study whether the April 1, 1992 minimum wage increase in New Jersey (from $4.25 to $5.05) reduced fast-food employment. They compare fast-food stores in New Jersey to stores in eastern Pennsylvania, where the minimum wage did not change.
The core empirical design is a difference-in-differences (DID): compare the change in employment in New Jersey before vs.┬Āafter the policy to the same before-after change in Pennsylvania.
Note on replication targets: the assignment emphasizes matching point estimates closely. Standard errors can differ across replications because of choices about sample restrictions and handling of temporarily closed stores.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Main Analysis

# Load and label the public data
path <- "data/raw/public.dat"
raw <- read.table(path, header = FALSE, na.strings = ".", strip.white = TRUE, fill = TRUE)

names(raw) <- c(
  "sheet","chain","co_owned","state","southj","centralj","northj","pa1","pa2","shore",
  "ncalls","empft","emppt","nmgrs","wage_st","inctime","firstinc","bonus","pctaff","meals",
  "open","hrsopen","psoda","pfry","pentree","nregs","nregs11","type2","status2","date2",
  "ncalls2","empft2","emppt2","nmgrs2","wage_st2","inctime2","firstin2","special2","meals2",
  "open2r","hrsopen2","psoda2","pfry2","pentree2","nregs2","nregs112"
)

d <- raw

d$nj <- d$state
d$emptot <- d$empft + 0.5 * d$emppt + d$nmgrs
d$emptot2 <- d$empft2 + 0.5 * d$emppt2 + d$nmgrs2
d$demp <- d$emptot2 - d$emptot
d$closed <- d$status2 == 3

d$pmeal <- d$psoda + d$pfry + d$pentree
d$pmeal2 <- d$psoda2 + d$pfry2 + d$pentree2

d$chain_bk <- as.integer(d$chain == 1)
d$chain_kfc <- as.integer(d$chain == 2)
d$chain_roys <- as.integer(d$chain == 3)
d$chain_wendys <- as.integer(d$chain == 4)

# GAP variable used in Card-Krueger regression specs
d$gap <- NA_real_
d$gap[d$state == 0] <- 0
idx <- which(d$state == 1 & !is.na(d$wage_st) & d$wage_st >= 5.05)
d$gap[idx] <- 0
idx <- which(d$state == 1 & !is.na(d$wage_st) & d$wage_st > 0 & d$wage_st < 5.05)
d$gap[idx] <- (5.05 - d$wage_st[idx]) / d$wage_st[idx]

mean_se <- function(x) {
  n <- sum(!is.na(x))
  c(mean = mean(x, na.rm = TRUE), se = sd(x, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n))
}


Replicate Selected Table 2 Moments

# A compact subset of Table 2 means (by state)
table2_subset <- data.frame(
  statistic = c(
    "Wave 1 FTE employment",
    "Wave 1 starting wage",
    "Wave 1 full meal price",
    "Wave 2 FTE employment",
    "Wave 2 starting wage",
    "Wave 2 full meal price"
  ),
  NJ = c(
    mean(d$emptot[d$nj == 1], na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(d$wage_st[d$nj == 1], na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(d$pmeal[d$nj == 1], na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(d$emptot2[d$nj == 1], na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(d$wage_st2[d$nj == 1], na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(d$pmeal2[d$nj == 1], na.rm = TRUE)
  ),
  PA = c(
    mean(d$emptot[d$nj == 0], na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(d$wage_st[d$nj == 0], na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(d$pmeal[d$nj == 0], na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(d$emptot2[d$nj == 0], na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(d$wage_st2[d$nj == 0], na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(d$pmeal2[d$nj == 0], na.rm = TRUE)
  )
)

table2_subset$NJ <- round(table2_subset$NJ, 2)
table2_subset$PA <- round(table2_subset$PA, 2)
table2_subset

               statistic    NJ    PA
1  Wave 1 FTE employment 20.44 23.33
2   Wave 1 starting wage  4.61  4.63
3 Wave 1 full meal price  3.35  3.04
4  Wave 2 FTE employment 21.03 21.17
5   Wave 2 starting wage  5.08  4.62
6 Wave 2 full meal price  3.41  3.03


These estimates align closely with Table 2 in the paper. For example, Wave 1 FTE employment is about 20.44 in New Jersey and 23.33 in Pennsylvania, indicating that the replicated summary statistics are consistent with the published results.


Replicate Table 3 (Rows 1-3, Columns 1-3)

# Table 3 rows 1-3, cols 1-3 interpreted as: All stores, NJ, PA
r1 <- c(
  all = mean(d$emptot, na.rm = TRUE),
  nj  = mean(d$emptot[d$nj == 1], na.rm = TRUE),
  pa  = mean(d$emptot[d$nj == 0], na.rm = TRUE)
)

r2 <- c(
  all = mean(d$emptot2, na.rm = TRUE),
  nj  = mean(d$emptot2[d$nj == 1], na.rm = TRUE),
  pa  = mean(d$emptot2[d$nj == 0], na.rm = TRUE)
)

r3 <- r2 - r1

replicated_t3 <- rbind(
  "1. FTE before" = r1,
  "2. FTE after" = r2,
  "3. Change" = r3
)

paper_t3 <- rbind(
  "1. FTE before" = c(all = 21.00, nj = 20.44, pa = 23.33),
  "2. FTE after" = c(all = 21.05, nj = 21.03, pa = 21.17),
  "3. Change" = c(all = 0.05, nj = 0.59, pa = -2.16)
)

comparison_t3 <- data.frame(
  row = rownames(replicated_t3),
  All_rep = round(replicated_t3[, "all"], 2),
  All_paper = paper_t3[, "all"],
  NJ_rep = round(replicated_t3[, "nj"], 2),
  NJ_paper = paper_t3[, "nj"],
  PA_rep = round(replicated_t3[, "pa"], 2),
  PA_paper = paper_t3[, "pa"]
)

comparison_t3

                        row All_rep All_paper NJ_rep NJ_paper PA_rep PA_paper
1. FTE before 1. FTE before   21.00     21.00  20.44    20.44  23.33    23.33
2. FTE after   2. FTE after   21.05     21.05  21.03    21.03  21.17    21.17
3. Change         3. Change    0.06      0.05   0.59     0.59  -2.17    -2.16


The replicated point estimates match the published values to two decimal places, which suggests that the data preparation and sample construction are consistent with the original analysis.


Replicate Table 4, Column (i)

# Follow the sample restriction in check.sas (357 stores)
c1 <- subset(d, !is.na(demp) & (closed == 1 | (closed == 0 & !is.na(wage_st2 - wage_st))))

m_col1 <- lm(demp ~ nj, data = c1)
co <- summary(m_col1)$coefficients

table4_col1 <- data.frame(
  term = rownames(co),
  estimate = round(co[, "Estimate"], 2),
  std_error = round(co[, "Std. Error"], 2)
)

table4_col1

                   term estimate std_error
(Intercept) (Intercept)    -2.13      1.07
nj                   nj     2.33      1.19

nrow(c1)

[1] 357


The New Jersey dummy is approximately 2.33 with standard error 1.19, matching Table 4 column (i).

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Something Additional (Difference-in-Differences Visualization)
I add a DID figure to visualize the main identification pattern by comparing New Jersey and Pennsylvania before and after the policy change.

# Balanced sample for a clean pre/post comparison
bal <- subset(d, !is.na(emptot) & !is.na(emptot2))

mean_pre_nj <- mean(bal$emptot[bal$nj == 1])
mean_post_nj <- mean(bal$emptot2[bal$nj == 1])
mean_pre_pa <- mean(bal$emptot[bal$nj == 0])
mean_post_pa <- mean(bal$emptot2[bal$nj == 0])

plot(
  x = c(1, 2), y = c(mean_pre_pa, mean_post_pa),
  type = "b", pch = 16, lwd = 2, col = "steelblue",
  xaxt = "n", xlab = "Survey Wave", ylab = "Average FTE Employment",
  ylim = range(c(mean_pre_nj, mean_post_nj, mean_pre_pa, mean_post_pa)),
  main = "Difference-in-Differences Pattern: NJ vs PA"
)
axis(1, at = c(1, 2), labels = c("Before (Wave 1)", "After (Wave 2)"))
lines(c(1, 2), c(mean_pre_nj, mean_post_nj), type = "b", pch = 17, lwd = 2, col = "firebrick")
legend("topright", legend = c("Pennsylvania", "New Jersey"), col = c("steelblue", "firebrick"), pch = c(16, 17), lwd = 2, bty = "n")








Interpretation: PA employment declines between waves, while NJ employment rises slightly. The difference in these trends is the DID estimate, consistent with the paperŌĆÖs conclusion that the minimum wage increase did not reduce fast-food employment in this case.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
References

Card, David and Alan B. Krueger (1994), Minimum Wages and Employment: A Case Study of the Fast-Food Industry in New Jersey and Pennsylvania, American Economic Review 84(4): 772-793.
Data source: https://davidcard.berkeley.edu/data_sets.html

title: From Ad Clicks to Customer Value
section: 01 A funnel connects ad clicks to real customer value
text:
01 A funnel connects ad clicks to real customer value
What is a marketing funnel?
A funnel describes the journey a customer takes ŌĆö from first seeing your ad to becoming a loyal, repeat buyer. At each stage, some people drop off, just like water leaking through a funnel.

The five stages

Reach ŌĆö Your ad is shown to a qualified audience
Click ŌĆö Someone clicks the ad, signaling interest
Activate ŌĆö The user experiences the productŌĆÖs value for the first time (e.g.┬Ācompletes sign-up, tries a feature)
Purchase ŌĆö The user pays, turning interest into revenue
Retain ŌĆö The user comes back again and again, building long-term value (also called CLV ŌĆö Customer Lifetime Value, the total revenue one customer brings over time)

title: From Ad Clicks to Customer Value
section: 02 The click problem: activity can look like growth
text:
02 The click problem: activity can look like growth
Why top-of-funnel metrics can mislead
Top-of-funnel means the early stages ŌĆö the metrics you see first, like clicks.

High CTR (Click-Through Rate) or low CPC (Cost Per Click) can make a campaign look efficient, even when most clicks never turn into real customers.
Traffic can increase while retention (customers who keep coming back) and payback (recovering your ad spend) remain poor.
The biggest leak isnŌĆÖt always visible at the top ŌĆö compare each stageŌĆÖs drop-off against past data or benchmarks to find it.


The key question shift

Clicks answer: Who reacted?
Funnel analysis answers: Who activated, retained, and created value?

title: From Ad Clicks to Customer Value
section: 03 Real example: Starbucks Rewards as a customer-value funnel
text:
03 Real example: Starbucks Rewards as a customer-value funnel





33.8M
U.S. 90-day active Rewards members (Q4 FY2024)


+4%
Year-over-year membership growth in Q4 FY2024


$36.2B
FY2024 consolidated net revenue


Source: Starbucks Q4 FY2024 results and FY2024 annual report.

title: From Ad Clicks to Customer Value
section: 04 Decision example: the best lever may not be more ads
text:
04 Decision example: the best lever may not be more ads
Illustrative funnel math



Stage
Users
Drop-off




Clicks
100,000
ŌĆö


Sign-ups
12,000
ŌłÆ88%


Activated
8,160
ŌłÆ32%


First purchases
3,753
ŌłÆ54%


Retained
3,003
ŌłÆ20%




Strategic options:

Sign-up conversion: Simplify registration and align ad copy with the landing page to reduce the drop-off from clicks to sign-ups.
First purchase conversion: After activation, introduce conversion nudges ŌĆö first-order discounts, limited-time offers, or personalized recommendations.

A/B testing across both stages helps identify which approach drives the best results.

title: From Ad Clicks to Customer Value
section: 05 Takeaway: optimize for customer equity, not campaign activity
text:
05 Takeaway: optimize for customer equity, not campaign activity

Clicks are a signal, not the destination.
Rank bottlenecks by value loss ŌĆö find the stage where the most revenue is escaping, not just the stage with the most visible drop.







The best marketers do not stop at ŌĆ£what converted?ŌĆØ
They ask: ŌĆ£what created customers worth keeping?ŌĆØ

title: Analysis of Cars
text:
LetŌĆÖs investigate the relationship between fuel efficiency (mpg) and engine displacement (disp) from the mtcars dataset. Those variables have a correlation of -0.85.


Here is a plot:

library(tidyverse)
data(mtcars)
mtcars |>
  ggplot(aes(mpg, disp)) + 
  geom_point(color="dodgerblue4", size=2)

mtcars |>
  ggplot(aes(mpg, disp)) + 
  geom_point(color="dodgerblue4", size=2)

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Blueprinty is a small software company that helps engineering firms prepare blueprints for patent applications. Its marketing team would like to make a strong claim: firms that use BlueprintyŌĆÖs software are more successful at getting patents awarded.
The cleanest way to study that claim would be a before-and-after design for the same firms, or even better, a randomized experiment in which some firms are assigned access to the software and others are not. That is not the data available here. Instead, Blueprinty has collected a cross-section of 1,500 mature engineering firms. For each firm, the dataset records the number of patents awarded in the last five years, the firmŌĆÖs region, its age since incorporation, and whether it is a Blueprinty customer.
That makes the analysis useful, but not automatic. BlueprintyŌĆÖs customers are not randomly selected. If customer firms are older, concentrated in more patent-intensive regions, or systematically different in ways we cannot observe, then a raw comparison of patent counts could confuse those firm characteristics with the effect of the software. The goal of this article is to move from a naive comparison to a Poisson regression model estimated by maximum likelihood, then translate the fitted model into an interpretable estimate of BlueprintyŌĆÖs association with patent awards.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Blueprinty is a small software company that helps engineering firms prepare blueprints for patent applications. Its marketing team would like to make a strong claim: firms that use BlueprintyŌĆÖs software are more successful at getting patents awarded.
The cleanest way to study that claim would be a before-and-after design for the same firms, or even better, a randomized experiment in which some firms are assigned access to the software and others are not. That is not the data available here. Instead, Blueprinty has collected a cross-section of 1,500 mature engineering firms. For each firm, the dataset records the number of patents awarded in the last five years, the firmŌĆÖs region, its age since incorporation, and whether it is a Blueprinty customer.
That makes the analysis useful, but not automatic. BlueprintyŌĆÖs customers are not randomly selected. If customer firms are older, concentrated in more patent-intensive regions, or systematically different in ways we cannot observe, then a raw comparison of patent counts could confuse those firm characteristics with the effect of the software. The goal of this article is to move from a naive comparison to a Poisson regression model estimated by maximum likelihood, then translate the fitted model into an interpretable estimate of BlueprintyŌĆÖs association with patent awards.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Exploring the Data
The outcome is a count: the number of patents awarded to each firm during the last five years. Customers have a visibly higher average count, but the distribution is right-skewed for both groups. Most firms receive only a few patents, while a small number receive many more.








Patent Awards by Customer Status


Status
Firms
Mean patents
Median patents
Firms with zero patents




Non-customer
1,019
3.47
3.00
5.9%


Blueprinty customer
481
4.13
4.00
5.0%












Patent counts are higher for Blueprinty customers, but both groups have skewed count distributions.




The average non-customer has 3.47 patents, while the average Blueprinty customer has 4.13. That raw difference points in the direction Blueprinty would like, but it is not yet evidence that the software caused the difference.
One reason is geography. Customer status is strongly related to region: Blueprinty customers are disproportionately located in the Northeast. If patenting intensity differs by region, the raw customer comparison will be confounded by where the firms are located.





Blueprinty customers are much more concentrated in the Northeast than non-customers.




Age is another potential confounder. Older firms may have larger engineering teams, deeper patent portfolios, and more experience navigating the patent office. Customer and non-customer age distributions are not identical, so the regression model below controls flexibly for age.








Firm Age by Customer Status


Status
Mean age
Median age

75th percentile

Blueprinty customer

26.5
20.5
32.5












Firm age is broadly similar across groups, but the regression will still adjust for age because age is related to patent output.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
section: A Simple Poisson Model via Maximum Likelihood
text:
A Simple Poisson Model via Maximum Likelihood
Because patents are non-negative integer counts, a Normal model is not a natural starting point. A Normal distribution can assign probability to impossible negative values and treats the outcome as continuous. The Poisson distribution is designed for counts, so it is the first model to try.
Start with the simplest possible version: every firm has the same patent-award rate ():
[ Y_i (). ]
For one firm, the probability mass function is
[ f(y_i )=. ]
Assuming the firms are independent and identically distributed under this simple model, the joint likelihood is the product of the individual probabilities:
[ L(; y_1,,y_n) =_{i=1}^n . ]
This simplifies to
[ L() =. ]
In practice, we work with the log-likelihood. It has the same maximizer as the likelihood, and it avoids multiplying many tiny probabilities:
[ () =L() =_{i=1}^n . ]
Here is the log-likelihood written directly in R.

poisson_loglikelihood <- function(lambda, Y) {
  if (length(lambda) != 1 || lambda <= 0) {
    return(-Inf)
  }

  sum(-lambda + Y * log(lambda) - lfactorial(Y))
}

lambda_grid <- seq(0.25, 7, length.out = 400)
loglik_grid <- data.frame(
  lambda = lambda_grid,
  log_likelihood = vapply(lambda_grid, poisson_loglikelihood, numeric(1), Y = Y)
)

simple_mle <- optim(
  par = sample_mean * 0.75,
  fn = function(lambda) -poisson_loglikelihood(lambda, Y),
  method = "L-BFGS-B",
  lower = 1e-8
)

lambda_hat_simple <- as.numeric(simple_mle$par)






The log-likelihood peaks at the sample mean, which is the MLE for a one-parameter Poisson model.












Simple Poisson MLE Check


Quantity
Value




Sample mean
3.684667


Numerical MLE from optim()
3.684667


Difference
0.000000







The visual MLE is the value of () at the peak of the curve. We can also derive it directly. The derivative of the log-likelihood is
[ =-n+. ]
Setting this equal to zero gives
[ _{MLE}=_i y_i={Y}. ]
That result feels right: the mean of a Poisson distribution is (), so the best-fitting single rate is the sample average number of patents, 3.685.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Poisson Regression
The one-rate model is useful for learning MLE, but it ignores the actual business question. Firms differ in age, region, and customer status. A Poisson regression lets each firm have its own expected patent count:
[ Y_i (_i), _i=(X_iŌĆÖ). ]
The exponential link matters because (_i) must be positive. The linear predictor (X_iŌĆÖ) can be any real number, but ((X_iŌĆÖ)) is always greater than zero.
For this model, the log-likelihood becomes
[ () =_{i=1}^n . ]
The design matrix includes an intercept, age, age squared, region indicators, and customer status. I include age squared because the relationship between age and patenting is unlikely to be perfectly linear. I include all but one region indicator because the intercept already represents the omitted baseline region; including every region dummy plus an intercept would create perfect collinearity.

X <- model.matrix(
  ~ age + I(age^2) + region + iscustomer,
  data = blueprinty
)

poisson_regression_loglikelihood <- function(beta, Y, X) {
  eta <- as.vector(X %*% beta)
  lambda <- exp(eta)

  sum(Y * eta - lambda - lfactorial(Y))
}

start <- rep(0, ncol(X))
start[1] <- log(mean(Y))

mle_regression <- optim(
  par = start,
  fn = function(beta) -poisson_regression_loglikelihood(beta, Y, X),
  method = "BFGS",
  hessian = TRUE,
  control = list(maxit = 10000, reltol = 1e-12)
)

beta_hat <- mle_regression$par
names(beta_hat) <- colnames(X)

# Since we minimized the negative log-likelihood, the Hessian is the
# observed information matrix. Its inverse is the covariance estimate.
vcov_mle <- solve(mle_regression$hessian)
se_mle <- sqrt(diag(vcov_mle))

glm_fit <- glm(
  patents ~ age + I(age^2) + region + iscustomer,
  family = poisson(link = "log"),
  data = blueprinty
)









Hand-Coded MLE vs. Poisson GLM

The hand-coded likelihood and glm() estimates agree to within 1.6, which is the main diagnostic that the likelihood was coded correctly. The standard errors also line up closely. Small last-decimal differences can occur because optim() uses a numerical Hessian, while glm() uses the standard machinery for generalized linear models.





The customer coefficient is positive even after controlling for age and region.




The age terms suggest a curved relationship: expected patent counts rise with firm age at first, but the negative squared term means the increase eventually flattens and turns down. The region coefficients are small relative to their uncertainty in this specification. The main coefficient of interest is iscustomer, which is positive and precisely estimated.
Because the model is log-linear, the customer coefficient is easiest to read after exponentiating:








Customer Status Effect on the Rate Scale


Quantity
Value




Customer coefficient
0.0424


Incidence rate ratio
1.043


Implied percent difference
4.3%


95% IRR interval
NA to NA







Holding age and region fixed, Blueprinty customers are estimated to have about 4.3% higher expected patent counts than otherwise similar non-customers.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
The Effect of BlueprintyŌĆÖs Software
The coefficient is useful, but it is still not in the most intuitive units. A coefficient of 0.042 means ŌĆ£higher log expected patents,ŌĆØ not ŌĆ£this many additional patents.ŌĆØ To translate the model into patent counts, I create two counterfactual datasets:

(X_0): every firm is treated as a non-customer.
(X_1): every firm is treated as a Blueprinty customer.

Age and region stay exactly as observed for each firm. Only customer status changes.

blueprinty_0 <- blueprinty
blueprinty_1 <- blueprinty

blueprinty_0$iscustomer <- 0
blueprinty_1$iscustomer <- 1

X0 <- model.matrix(~ age + I(age^2) + region + iscustomer, data = blueprinty_0)
X1 <- model.matrix(~ age + I(age^2) + region + iscustomer, data = blueprinty_1)

yhat0 <- as.vector(exp(X0 %*% beta_hat))
yhat1 <- as.vector(exp(X1 %*% beta_hat))

firm_lift <- yhat1 - yhat0
average_effect <- mean(firm_lift)









Counterfactual Expected Patent Counts


Scenario
Mean expected patents over five years




All firms set to non-customer
3.679


All firms set to Blueprinty customer
3.839


Average customer effect
0.159












The model implies larger absolute gains for firms with higher baseline expected patent counts.




The fitted model estimates that being a Blueprinty customer is associated with an average increase of about 0.16 patents over five years, holding each firmŌĆÖs age and region fixed. This is the most direct version of BlueprintyŌĆÖs marketing claim in the units the business cares about.
The careful interpretation is still observational. The model adjusts for age and region, and after those adjustments the customer association remains positive. But it does not prove that the software caused the increase. Customer firms may differ in unobserved ways: they may have larger R&D budgets, stronger legal teams, more aggressive patent strategies, or a greater tendency to buy tools that support patent applications. The evidence is consistent with BlueprintyŌĆÖs claim, but a randomized rollout or credible before-and-after design would be needed for a stronger causal conclusion.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Conclusion
Maximum likelihood estimation provides the bridge from a probability model to an empirical answer. The simple Poisson model shows how the MLE selects the rate that makes the observed patent counts most likely. The regression model applies the same logic with a richer rate parameter, (_i=(X_iŌĆÖ)), so that firms can differ by age, region, and customer status.
In this dataset, Blueprinty customers receive more patents on average, and that positive relationship remains after controlling for observed firm characteristics. The estimated association is about 4.3% higher expected patent counts, or roughly 0.16 additional patents over five years for the average firm in the sample.
